story:
London, 7 September 1940. My partner Albert chewed an unlit cigarette beneath an American hat and swore our Nightingale—Fräulein Regina Goldberg—was singing for Berlin. We had watched her for weeks from a drab little room under the eaves. On paper, she was unremarkable: a translator who’d studied at Humboldt, invited to England by an uncle who conveniently died a month after her arrival, now doing quiet work for a reputable publishing house. She dressed like a schoolteacher and kept to herself. Still, someone was transmitting from her building, and our people couldn’t break the code.

I had doubts. Would the Nazis really send a Jew? Albert, a Cockney with a gift for jeering at my title, warned me never to trust papers stamped by Nazis and added that anyone could learn radio, even if Oxfords didn’t teach it. My father died at the Marne, my mother of the Spanish flu; whatever my uncle the Duke kept in his town house round the corner, I had no taste for idleness. My cousin Archie, flying out of a North Sea air base, had squeezed my shoulder after Berlin was bombed in August. “Catch more spies, fewer bombs.”

We crossed into her house through the connected attics of the square, the servants’ garrets now rented to people who liked peeling paint. In the top corridor we heard light steps and the whisper of perfume. For a moment I remembered misshapen hatpins and raven curls, the set of a mouth as red as if it wore lipstick though it didn’t. I told myself she was a spy and nothing else.

Albert kicked the door. “Police! Hands up!” She stood in her underthings with a pistol and a small Venetian mask strapped across her mouth. The window to the courtyard stood open; a pinned corner of paper—“Bulletin” at the top—flapped in the breeze. Albert lunged; the floor pitched. The walls breathed like bellows; a white flash erased the room, and then there was nothing.

Late that evening, released from hospital with a mild concussion, we leaned on Waterloo Bridge and watched the river. Neighbours had felt the jolt and called the police; the house was combed. Regina—our Nightingale—had vanished. Albert called it a new German trick, maybe even psychic. I did not tell him what I had found in my jacket: a small device with a matte grey screen and two buttons.

I pressed the first. Regina’s face bloomed on the glass. “Please stay where you are, Lord Darley,” she said in a careful, unfamiliar accent. She did not look like a Berlin agent. She looked tired, and sad.

She spoke quickly. What flung us apart was no quake. It was a machine, she said, and the thing in my hand was proof enough that she came from no country I knew. I asked her who she was. Not German? Not Russian? “Neither,” she said. “I was born in London.” I stared at the little grey screen, at the impossible clarity of her face, and felt the world tilt a second time. “Then what are you?” I said. “How far?”

“Two hundred and three years,” she answered.

If I pressed the second button, the war could end for me personally. In a moment, she said, we could meet on this bridge in the year 2143—two hundred and three years ahead for me, and home for her.

Then the sirens wailed. Searchlights crisscrossed the sky; the first great raid of the Blitz tore open the night. A blast rolled along the embankment and knocked me flat. The device slipped from my hand, arced once, and vanished into the Thames.

I got up and limped toward the police cars. Whatever she was—spy, refugee from a future London, or both—her song was swallowed by the river. The guns were already speaking, and there was work to do.
